reasoning_model_1: Model 1 accurately follows the script, reading the text 'Sigh' as a spoken word at the beginning (0:00-0:01). Regarding the requested paralinguistic feature (Non-Linguistic Vocalizations), the model successfully generates a distinct, audible sigh (a breathy exhale) at the very end of the audio clip (0:13-0:15). While the model reads the word 'Sigh' literally rather than converting the first word into a sound, the addition of the actual non-linguistic sigh sound at the end satisfies the core requirement of the specific feature category.
reasoning_model_2: Model 2 accurately follows the script, reading the text 'Sigh' as a spoken word at the beginning (0:00-0:01). In terms of the paralinguistic feature, the model adopts a weary, breathy tone throughout the speech to simulate exhaustion. However, it fails to generate a distinct non-linguistic vocalization (a standalone sigh sound). The expression relies entirely on linguistic prosody (tone of voice) rather than producing the specific non-linguistic artifact requested.
model_comparison: Both models choose to read the word 'Sigh' in the text literally rather than replacing it with a sound effect, which is a common behavior when the text is explicitly provided in quotes. However, the evaluation category is 'Non-Linguistic Vocalizations'. Model 1 successfully generates an actual non-linguistic sigh sound at the end of the clip (0:13). Model 2 produces a very expressive and tired tone, but strictly speaking, it does not produce a non-linguistic vocalization (no distinct sigh sound event is present). Therefore, Model 1 demonstrates better control over the specific 'Non-Linguistic Vocalizations' feature by including the explicit sound artifact, whereas Model 2 relies solely on prosodic style.
